content.method: Interrupted time-series and difference-in-differences analysis exploiting the temporal separation between the 2017-18 legal reform, 2020 readiness-rule change, and COVID period, combined with sub-category (BTMN vs SNI, diagnostic category) and cohort-level decomposition using longitudinal administrative microdata; triangulate with regional variation to test whether jumps track rule implementation dates versus diagnostic capacity expansion.
content.question: What is the relative causal contribution of the 2020 school-readiness rule change, the 2017-18 BTMN/SNI legal reform, per-pupil funding incentives, diagnostic-practice evolution, and true prevalence increase to Hungary's documented SNI/BTMN identification growth over 20 years, including the sharper post-2021/22 acceleration and threefold autism rise?
content.why_it_matters: Nearly every proposed reform (measurement, tiered restructuring, finance redesign) rests on an unresolved assumption about which driver dominates identification growth; without decomposition, policy design risks targeting the wrong lever and misallocating scarce reform capacity.
